Okay, now we own them all. So all roads lead to wrong, right? There's nothing disreputable about that, or we could reference it. So John Duquesne, marketing experts piece shows up and says, I happen to know you were at the success event in Dufas Arena. I was there too, right? And I'll bet you're fascinated with how to market your business better story, but boy, that guy was an asshole. And you know, but if you're really interested in that stuff, let me tell you my story. So we could reference it, just like the Monday night bailout call references it. There's a lot of money there. And so if you're dealing in small numbers, not so much. If you're dealing in big numbers, lots of leads, much. Last, rent, sell, exchange, barter your names. Now again, the more names, the more significant this is. But even at a small level, it can be significant. So if you generate leads and they cost you a buck a piece, and she generate leads and they cost a buck a piece, and when you swap your used leads, they're worth a half a buck to you, right? You just got a half a buck for nothing, right? Now you can use that to put in your pocket, or now you can go spend a buck and a half to get every lead, which now allows you to spend more money per lead. If four of you exchange them, you can now spend whatever that is too. So yeah, you spend three bucks a lead now, where before you could only spend a buck. So swapping in exchange and leads can have significant economic impact. If you generate a lot of leads and you have big lists, there's a lot of money to be made in a rental marketplace. So let's say you're going to generate a bunch of leads. And we know, therefore, you're going to have a bunch of unconverted leads, especially early. Now again, if you only generate a little bit of leads, this is not so rough. But if you generate a lot of leads, this matters. So if you generate a lot of leads and you're going to have a lot of unconverted leads, some of those unconverted leads are more likely to convert later than others. Therefore, it's in our best interest to be able to treat them differently, not to treat them all the same. So this is not about being fair, just, and democratic. This is about being discriminatory. So if Susan is more likely to buy from me than Steven, I want to send Susan better stuff. I want to send her more stuff. And I want to send it to her in a better way. And if he's less so, they would just tell me. So Susan would say, I'm not buying right now, but hey, I'm really likely to buy from you in the next month. And Steven would tell me, you haven't got a prayer selling to me. I ain't ever buying. That would be really helpful, because I would treat them differently. Unfortunately, people won't tell us this. And sometimes they don't know, but often they do. So we were generating a lot of leads with that television show and with print ads and with direct mail for Go By The Engine. So we wanted to figure out a way to have the leads tell us whether they were good leads or better leads, so we could treat them differently. So this is primitive, of course, but here's what we did. So this went in with the first package sent to the lead to sell them their thing. And it said, you can go ahead and buy the starter kit, in which case you will get the videotape with it for free. But if you're not going to buy the starter kit and you would like to see the video, send me $10. Guess what? Crapload of people sent $10. So they got the whole package, they read the whole package, they didn't buy, but they put $10 in an envelope with a coupon and sent it to be sent to video, which clearly is a videotape selling them the thing they're not buying. So if there's 1,000 leads and 100 of them send in this thing with $10, it ain't about $1,000, by the way, although it's all cash. Statue of limitations has run out, so I can tell you it's a fairly safe wager. He wasn't exactly calling the feds every day and telling them how many $10 bills came in the mail. There's a certain, even psychic satisfaction to that, even if it doesn't amount to much. But so now we got 100 people who weren't ready to buy yet for whatever reason, but they gave us $10 to hear some more. And we got 900 people who didn't give us $10. We can sick the tele-shark. Who are we going to sick the tele-sharks on first? Of course, the guys just said the $10. Who do we want to pack a bunch of stuff in? And FedEx, who do we want to send direct mail to only, or now, who might we only send email to? Here's the differentiator. By the way, it ran between 15% and 20%, which, you know, surprised me, but nevertheless, clever. Drop down sale. Again, one of the reasons is price, right? Actual inability or emotional inability to pay the ask for price. So we're always going to have more unconverted leads, and we have converted leads. So in almost every case, it's pretty damn stupid. And it may be economic suicide not to have a way that they can buy something less expensive at some point to get their toe in the water and get in the game. Because a big hunk of them didn't buy only because of price. You had them until price. So if we give them an opportunity to do something for less money, they'll do it. What you always want to do is you always want to loop back up built into this process. So if you're selling a $1,000 thing, and at some point you let them buy a miniaturized version of the $1,000 thing, or a little hunk of the $1,000 thing for $200, you want to loop back up to the $1,000 thing, and they get full credit for the $200 they spent. And depending upon your economics, you give them double credit for the $200 they spent if they do it within a certain period of time. And again, if you had telesharks, who would you hand to the teleshark? Non-buyers or drop-down buyers, you would hand drop-down buyers because they're better prospects. So the drop-down sale is a big economic factor. I gave you the go-by-the-inch drop-down letter. So your next few pages are the drop-down letter, which was from when we started $3.99, when we ended $8.99, and the drop-down was to $9.99. And there's a loop back up. And I have a dinner break, and I have only one leftover for tomorrow, so that's pretty good.
